statement.p: A river flows at 13 miles per hour. A boat traveled with the current from Town A to Town B, which are 280 miles apart. Then, the boat turned around, and traveled against the current to reach Town C, which is 120 miles away from Town B. The second leg of the trip (Town B to Town C) took the same time as the first leg (Town A to Town B). During this whole trip, the boat was driving at a constant still-water speed. Answer the following question:
During this trip, the boats speed on still water was miles per hour.

solution.p: Assume that the boats still-water speed was x miles per hour.

Since the currents speed was 13 miles per hour, the boat traveled from Town A to Town B (with the current) at x+13 miles per hour, and from Town B to Town C (against the current) at x-13 miles per hour.

Using the formula \text{time}=\frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}, we know the boat traveled from Town A to Town B in \frac{280}{x+13} hours; and it traveled from Town B to Town C in \frac{120}{x-13} hours.
Its given that those two legs took the same amount of time, so we can write an equation and solve it:
The boats still-water speed was 32.5 miles per hour.
